trajectory.description: Observation: Starting from scratch with 2 hours and 30 iterations. Need to build a 3D GNN for HOMO-LUMO gap prediction. Analysis: 3D geometric methods achieve much better MAE (~0.03-0.06 eV) than SMILES methods. PyG is available, so I'll implement a custom SchNet-style model for reliability. Decision: Write model.py, train.py, predict.py. Train for 3 epochs on iteration 0 to establish baseline and measure pipeline speed.

Observation: Iteration 1 crashed with exit code 139 (segfault) after 960s with no stdout. Analysis: Exit code 139 in PyTorch+PyG almost always stems from DataLoader multiprocessing (num_workers > 0) forking after CUDA initialization. The child processes inherit corrupted CUDA contexts and segfault. Decision: Remove num_workers (set to 0) in both train.py and predict.py. Run a minimal 1-epoch test to verify the pipeline works and measure iteration time before scaling up.

trajectory.command:
python -c "
import torch
from model import PureSchNet
m = PureSchNet(hidden_channels=64, num_interactions=2)
z = torch.randint(1,10,(50,))
pos = torch.randn(50,3)
batch = torch.cat([torch.zeros(20,dtype=torch.long), torch.ones(30,dtype=torch.long)])
out = m(z,pos,batch)
print('Forward OK', out.shape)
" && python train.py --epochs 1 --max_samples 1000 --hidden 64 --interactions 2
trajectory.error_summary: bash: line 10:   315 Segmentation fault      (core dumped) python train.py --epochs 1 --max_samples 1000 --hidden 64 --interactions 2
trajectory.description: Observation: Two consecutive iterations segfaulted (exit 139) after ~800-960s with empty stdout. The custom SchNet model uses PyG's radius_graph and MessagePassing C++ extensions (torch_cluster), which are the most common source of silent CUDA segfaults in PyG. Analysis: To eliminate the C++ extension risk, I will replace the sparse graph radius_graph approach with a pure-PyTorch dense/loop model. Since QM9 molecules are tiny (~18 atoms), fully-connected pairwise message passing inside a Python loop over batch graphs is fast enough and completely avoids torch_cluster. Decision: Rewrite model.py as PureSchNet using torch.cdist and pure PyTorch ops (no radius_graph, no MessagePassing). Update train.py/predict.py accordingly. First run a tiny forward pass + 1000-sample training to verify no segfault and basic functionality.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 segfaulted again (exit 139) during train.py, despite using a pure-PyTorch model and num_workers=0. The inline forward test passed, so the crash occurs inside the training loop. Primary suspect: torch.bincount on a CUDA tensor (only works on CPU in many PyTorch versions and can segfault). Additional risk: torch_geometric DataLoader may still invoke compiled C++ code during collate. Analysis: I will eliminate both risks by (1) vectorizing the model across the batch with padding/masking, removing the need for torch.bincount and per-sample Python loops, and (2) replacing PyG DataLoader with standard torch.utils.data.DataLoader and a custom collate function. This should be both safer and much faster. Decision: Rewrite model.py, train.py, and predict.py with fully-vectorized batching and pure PyTorch ops. Run a 1-epoch test on 2,000 samples to confirm no segfault and measure speed before scaling up.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 4 succeeded with a pure-PyTorch vectorized model (no segfault) on 2,000 samples. Val MAE 1.078 after 1 epoch with a tiny model. Analysis: The dense pairwise approach is safe and fast enough for QM9-sized molecules. We need to scale to full data, increase model capacity, and get a real eval baseline. Decision: Update model.py to apply distance cutoff masking (more physically correct), rewrite train.py to guarantee completeness, train on full data for 3 epochs with hidden=256/interactions=6, and submit eval immediately to measure end-to-end time and get a score.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 5 achieved a solid baseline with MAE 0.2845 using a simple dense SchNet (256 hidden, 6 interactions, 3 epochs). Training is extremely fast (~22 s/epoch) and stable with pure PyTorch. Analysis: We are far from the SOTA range (0.03–0.06 eV) primarily because the model is tiny and under-trained. A larger model with better inductive biases (cosine cutoff, residual updates, LayerNorm, atom-wise readout) and longer training (50 epochs) should substantially improve the score. Decision: Rewrite model.py with an improved InteractionBlock architecture, increase capacity to hidden=512, and train for 50 epochs. Then submit eval. This should fit comfortably in the remaining ~85 minutes.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 6 achieved MAE 0.1124 with a simple dense SchNet (512 hidden, 6 interactions, 50 epochs, batch_size=128). Training is fast (~30s/epoch) and stable with pure PyTorch. The model is still slowly improving at epoch 50, and the train-val gap is small (0.100 vs 0.114), suggesting longer training and architectural improvements will help without overfitting. Analysis: To push toward SOTA (0.03-0.06 eV), I will improve the architecture with learnable RBF widths, gated continuous filters, pre/post LayerNorm, atom-wise layers between interactions, and a sum+mean readout. I will also scale batch size to 512 for faster training and train for up to 100 epochs with early stopping (patience=20). Decision: Rewrite model.py and train.py with these improvements. Keep predict.py compatible. Train for 100 epochs and submit eval.
